perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Proprietary Information" shall mean written, documentary or oral information of any kind disclosed by Cyberlux or ICT to the other and designated as proprietary information, including, but not limited to, (a) information of a business, planning, marketing or technical nature, (b) models, tools, hardware and software, and (c) any documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party that contain, summarize or are based upon any Proprietary Information, provided that "Proprietary Information" shall not include information which: (i) is publicly available prior to the date of this Agreement; (ii) becomes publicly available after the date of this Agreement trough no wrongful act of the receiving party; (iii) is furnished to others by the disclosing party without similar restrictions on their right to use or disclose; (iv) is known by the receiving party without any proprietary restrictions at the time of receipt of such information from the disclosing party or becomes rightfully known to the receiving party without proprietary restrictions from a source other than the disclosing party; (v) is independently developed by the receiving party by persons who did not have access, directly or indirectly, to the proprietary Information; or /1/ (vi) is obligated to be produced under order of a court of competent jurisdiction or a valid administrative or congressional subpoena, provided that the receiving party promptly notifies the disclosing party of such event so that the disclosing party may seek an appropriate protective order or waive compliance by the receiving party with the terns of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Proprietary Information" shall mean written, documentary or oral information of any kind disclosed by Cyberlux or ICT to the other and designated as proprietary information, including, but not limited to, (a) information of a business, planning, marketing or technical nature, (b) models, tools, hardware and software, and (c) any documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party that contain, summarize or are based upon any Proprietary Information, provided that "Proprietary Information" shall not include information which: (i) is publicly available prior to the date of this Agreement; (ii) becomes publicly available after the date of this Agreement trough no wrongful act of the receiving party; (iii) is furnished to others by the disclosing party without similar restrictions on their right to use or disclose; (iv) is known by the receiving party without any proprietary restrictions at the time of receipt of such information from the disclosing party or becomes rightfully known to the receiving party without proprietary restrictions from a source other than the disclosing party; (v) is independently developed by the receiving party by persons who did have minimal access, directly or indirectly, to the proprietary Information; or /1/ (vi) is obligated to be produced under order of a court of competent jurisdiction or a valid administrative or congressional subpoena, provided that the receiving party promptly notifies the disclosing party of such event so that the disclosing party may seek an appropriate protective order or waive compliance by the receiving party with the terns of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'did not have access' to 'did have minimal access' regarding independently developed information contradicts established understanding of independent development.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the 'independent development' exception by suggesting that even minimal access to proprietary information doesn't necessarily negate independent development. This conflicts with trade secret law, which requires true independence in development to avoid misappropriation claims.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The change from 'did not have access' to 'did have minimal access' weakens the independent development exception. While the provided law snippet (18 USC 1839) primarily focuses on defining terms like 'foreign instrumentality' and 'foreign agent,' which are not directly relevant here, the Law Explanation clarifies that this change conflicts with trade secret law, which demands true independence in development. By allowing for 'minimal access,' the revised contract undermines this requirement, potentially leading to misappropriation claims. The perturbation explanation confirms that there is a contradiction as well. The modification therefore introduces a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of ten (10) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to ten years creates an unreasonable restriction on the use of information, potentially conflicting with trade secret law's requirement of ongoing efforts to maintain secrecy and North Carolina's statutes of limitations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations for Trade Secret Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: North Carolina General Statute § 1-52 outlines a 3-year statute of limitations for many claims. While trade secrets might have a different limitation period depending on the specific claim, extending the confidentiality period in the NDA to 10 years could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade if the information does not legitimately qualify for trade secret protection for that entire duration under applicable state law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract limits disclosure of proprietary information to three years. The modified contract extends this period to ten years. The Perturbation Explanation states this extension could create an unreasonable restriction, potentially conflicting with trade secret law and statutes of limitations. The Law Explanation supports this, mentioning North Carolina General Statute § 1-52's 3-year statute of limitations and suggesting the extended confidentiality period could be an unreasonable restraint of trade if the information does not qualify for trade secret protection for the entire duration. While specific statute details are missing from the scraped snippets, the explanations provided argue that the extended period is an unreasonable restraint of trade and introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information. Each party represents that such degree of care provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall endeavor to maintain the other party's Proprietary Information, but is not liable for security breaches outside of its direct control.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific standard of care tied to the party's own proprietary information and adding a liability disclaimer weakens the protection of proprietary information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care in Trade Secret Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: This change conflicts with the general duty of care required in protecting trade secrets. While there's no specific statute for duty of care, the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 40 establishes that maintaining trade secret protection involves reasonable efforts. The modified clause weakens this expectation by excusing liability for breaches outside direct control, potentially undermining reasonable security measures.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies a standard of care based on how each party protects its own proprietary information. The changed text removes this specific standard and introduces a liability disclaimer for security breaches outside of direct control. The Law Explanation states this change weakens the general duty of care required in protecting trade secrets, referencing the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 40, which suggests maintaining trade secret protection involves 'reasonable efforts.' While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation and law explanation highlight that removing the explicit standard of care and adding a liability disclaimer undermines the expected reasonable security measures, therefore contradicting the general duty of care required in protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each receiving party further acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for .any such breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each receiving party further acknowledges that damages may not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives. Such remedy shall be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for .any such breach of this Agreement, and are in lieu of all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'damages would not be' to 'damages may not be' adequate, and further stipulating that 'injunctive relief shall be the exclusive remedy' directly restricts the range of available legal remedies. This change limits the available remedies and thus can be viewed as an unconscionable limitation of liability and may not be enforceable under contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original contract preserves all available remedies; the modified text limits the disclosing party exclusively to injunctive relief. Under UCC § 2-719 (applicable in principle to contracts beyond just the sale of goods), limiting remedies in an unconscionable manner is prohibited. If the circumstances surrounding the breach render injunctive relief inadequate, the limitation of remedies may be deemed unenforceable, restoring the full range of remedies.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language significantly alters the remedies available to the disclosing party in the event of a breach. The original text explicitly states that injunctive relief is *in addition* to all other remedies, while the changed text limits the disclosing party to *exclusive* injunctive relief, and are in lieu of all other remedies available at law or in equity. The Law Explanation states this is an unconscionable limitation of liability and may not be enforceable under contract law, referencing UCC § 2-719. Although no specific law snippet is provided to directly corroborate this, the explanation coupled with the change in the original text, strongly implies that the range of available remedies has been restricted. This contradicts the original contract's intention to preserve all available legal avenues for the disclosing party, suggesting a potentially unenforceable limitation of liability.
